lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Come, poor remains of friends, rest on this rock.

lines.speaker: CLITUS
lines.text: Statilius show’d the torch-light, but, my lord,

lines.speaker: CLITUS
lines.text: He came not back: he is or ta’en or slain.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Sit thee down, Clitus: slaying is the word;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: It is a deed in fashion. Hark thee, Clitus.

lines.speaker: CLITUS
lines.text: What, I, my lord? No, not for all the world.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Peace then! no words.

lines.speaker: CLITUS
lines.text: I’ll rather kill myself.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Hark thee, Dardanius.

lines.speaker: DARDANIUS
lines.text: Shall I do such a deed?

lines.speaker: CLITUS
lines.text: O Dardanius!

lines.speaker: DARDANIUS
lines.text: O Clitus!

lines.speaker: CLITUS
lines.text: What ill request did Brutus make to thee?

lines.speaker: DARDANIUS
lines.text: To kill him, Clitus. Look, he meditates.

lines.speaker: CLITUS
lines.text: Now is that noble vessel full of grief,

lines.speaker: CLITUS
lines.text: That it runs over even at his eyes.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Come hither, good Volumnius; list a word.

lines.speaker: VOLUMNIUS
lines.text: What says my lord?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Why, this, Volumnius:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: The ghost of Caesar hath appear’d to me

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Two several times by night; at Sardis once,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And, this last night, here in Philippi fields:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I know my hour is come.

lines.speaker: VOLUMNIUS
lines.text: Not so, my lord.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Nay, I am sure it is, Volumnius.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Thou seest the world, Volumnius, how it goes;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Our enemies have beat us to the pit: Low alarums.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: It is more worthy to leap in ourselves,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Than tarry till they push us. Good Volumnius,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Thou know’st that we two went to school together:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Even for that our love of old, I prithee,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Hold thou my sword-hilts, whilst I run on it.

lines.speaker: VOLUMNIUS
lines.text: That’s not an office for a friend, my lord.

lines.speaker: CLITUS
lines.text: Fly, fly, my lord; there is no tarrying here.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Farewell to you; and you; and you, Volumnius.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Strato, thou hast been all this while asleep;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Farewell to thee too, Strato. Countrymen,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: My heart doth joy that yet in all my life

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I found no man but he was true to me.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I shall have glory by this losing day

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: More than Octavius and Mark Antony

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: By this vile conquest shall attain unto.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: So fare you well at once; for Brutus’ tongue

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Hath almost ended his life’s history:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Night hangs upon mine eyes; my bones would rest,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: That have but labour’d to attain this hour.

lines.speaker: CLITUS
lines.text: Fly, my lord, fly.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Hence! I will follow.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I prithee, Strato, stay thou by thy lord:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Thou art a fellow of a good respect;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Thy life hath had some smatch of honour in it:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Hold then my sword, and turn away thy face,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: While I do run upon it. Wilt thou, Strato?

lines.speaker: STRATO
lines.text: Give me your hand first. Fare you well, my lord.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Farewell, good Strato. Runs on his sword. Caesar, now be still:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I kill’d not thee with half so good a will.

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: What man is that?

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: My master’s man. Strato, where is thy master?

lines.speaker: STRATO
lines.text: Free from the bondage you are in, Messala:

lines.speaker: STRATO
lines.text: The conquerors can but make a fire of him;

lines.speaker: STRATO
lines.text: For Brutus only overcame himself,

lines.speaker: STRATO
lines.text: And no man else hath honour by his death.

lines.speaker: LUCILIUS
lines.text: So Brutus should be found. I thank thee, Brutus,

lines.speaker: LUCILIUS
lines.text: That thou hast proved Lucilius’ saying true.

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: All that served Brutus, I will entertain them.

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: Fellow, wilt thou bestow thy time with me?

lines.speaker: STRATO
lines.text: Ay, if Messala will prefer me to you.

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: Do so, good Messala.

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: How died my master, Strato?

lines.speaker: STRATO
lines.text: I held the sword, and he did run on it.

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: Octavius, then take him to follow thee,

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: That did the latest service to my master.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: This was the noblest Roman of them all:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: All the conspirators save only he

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Did that they did in envy of great Caesar;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: He only, in a general honest thought

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And common good to all, made one of them.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: His life was gentle, and the elements

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: So mix’d in him that Nature might stand up

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And say to all the world “This was a man!”

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: According to his virtue let us use him,

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: With all respect and rites of burial.

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: Within my tent his bones to-night shall lie,

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: Most like a soldier, order’d honourably.

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: So call the field to rest; and let’s away,

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: To part the glories of this happy day.

stage_directions.text: Enter Brutus, Dardanius, Clitus, Strato, and Volumnius.
Alarum. Retreat. Enter Octavius, Antony, Messala, Lucilius, and the army.

glosses.definition: the few friends left; the remnant
Statilius signalled with a torch that the other wing of the army was still whole — his failure to return meant the news was bad
killing is the order of the day
noble man filled with grief — Clitus's image for Brutus as a vessel overflowing

glosses.phrase: ghost of Caesar hath appear'd to me
glosses.definition: Caesar's Ghost, which Brutus first saw in 4.3, has returned — at Philippi, as the Ghost foretold. The prophecy is fulfilled
the visitation in 4.3 at Sardis

glosses.phrase: I know my hour is come
glosses.definition: I know my death is at hand — the Stoic's acceptance
driven us into the pit like hunted animals — the boar-hunt metaphor

glosses.phrase: more worthy to leap in ourselves / Than tarry till they push us
glosses.definition: more honourable to kill ourselves than wait for the enemy to do it — Brutus's revised position on suicide, abandoning 5.1's stance
the Roman suicide method: a friend holds the sword while you run onto the point. A ritualised, honourable death
honour from this day of defeat — Brutus's final Stoic claim: honour lies in the manner of losing
base victory — Brutus dismisses the triumvirs' win as dishonourable
a taste of honour; some measure of it
Brutus's suicide, achieved with Strato's help — the model Roman death: honourable, voluntary, assisted by a faithful servant
Caesar, now rest quiet — Brutus addresses Caesar's spirit; his death lays the ghost

glosses.phrase: I kill'd not thee with half so good a will
glosses.definition: I killed you with less willingness than I now kill myself — the play's most devastating half-line; Brutus's admission at the end that Caesar's murder was the harder deed
Brutus alone defeated Brutus — Strato's epitaph; honourable self-conquest
recommend me to your service

Antony's epitaph for Brutus — the play's most famous closing tribute. But note: Antony is consolidating power even while eulogizing; the generosity is also political theatre, binding former enemies to the new regime
out of jealousy of Caesar — Antony exempts Brutus alone from this charge, preserving the myth of the idealistic assassin
an honest concern for the general good
the four elements (earth, water, air, fire) were thought to compose the human constitution; 'well-mixed' meant temperamentally balanced; a Renaissance paean to the ideal man

here was a true man — Antony's closing valuation. Within fifteen years of this speech (by 27 BCE), the young Octavius will have become Augustus, first emperor; the Republic the conspirators sought to preserve will be gone. Antony's generosity to Brutus coincides with the Republic's end
proper funeral ceremonies
arranged with honour; a soldier's funeral
divide the spoils of victory — the triumvirs (eventually just Octavius) will carve up the Roman world. The play ends with the partition that makes Augustan rule possible

annotations.title: Brutus's suicide: the Stoic death earned
annotations.body:
Brutus's suicide on Strato's sword is Shakespeare's most fully-earned Roman death. The key elements are all present: the friend who holds the sword (Strato, like Cassius's Pindarus), the covered face (Strato turns his face away), the famous last word to the enemy ('Caesar, now be still: / I kill'd not thee with half so good a will'). Each is from the Roman suicide tradition Plutarch records for both Brutus and Cassius.

The final line — 'I kill'd not thee with half so good a will' — is Shakespeare's most devastating half-line in the play. Brutus admits, at the moment of his own death, that killing Caesar was harder for him than killing himself. The admission reverses the ideological framing. Through five acts Brutus has insisted that the assassination was a civic duty rendered with reluctant love; at the instant of his death he tells Caesar's spirit that he would rather be dead than have killed him. The Stoic case for the murder — that Brutus acted from general principle and private love — collapses into a simpler truth: he killed the man he loved and could not live with having done it.

Shakespeare does not underline this. He lets the line stand. But it is the play's judgment on the assassination that Brutus has finally offered, and it is the judgment he could not offer while living. The tragedy is that the clarity was available only at the cost of the life that might have acted on it.

Brutus's revised position on suicide, also worth noting: in 5.1 L101–108 he called Cato's suicide 'cowardly and vile.' Here he does it, and frames it as 'glory by this losing day.' The philosophical inconsistency is not a defect of Brutus's; it is the play's argument. Theoretical Stoicism cannot survive Philippi; only the practical Stoicism of the suicide itself remains.

annotations.title: 'This was the noblest Roman of them all': the epitaph as political theatre
annotations.body:
Antony's closing eulogy — 'This was the noblest Roman of them all' — is read at the play's end as pure tribute. In context it is also consolidation. Antony has just finished defeating the last republican army; the triumvirate has been proscribing senators; Octavius is standing beside him, preparing to eclipse him. Eulogising Brutus as singular among the conspirators ('all the conspirators save only he / Did that they did in envy of great Caesar') is politically shrewd. It separates Brutus from Cassius (dead), from Casca (dead), from Decius and the rest (dead or fugitive), and preserves Brutus's reputation as an ideal that the new regime can honour without being threatened by.

The technique matters. A defeated enemy, honoured, is a precedent for how the new regime will treat former opponents. Antony's language — 'His life was gentle, and the elements / So mix'd in him that Nature might stand up / And say to all the world, This was a man!' — is warm, specific, and famously generous. It is also a political statement. The new regime honours noble losers; former conspirators who yield can expect mercy; the coming empire has room for republican memory as long as republican action is ended.

Octavius's response ('With all respect and rites of burial') completes the move. The funeral will be in his tent. The burial is a performance of continuity: the man who killed Caesar will lie in state under the care of Caesar's heir. The gesture is political theatre of the highest order. It also definitively ends the Republic. The man who is honouring Brutus is the man who will, within fifteen years, rule Rome alone. The Republic the conspirators died to preserve is buried, with republican honours, by the first emperor.
sources: Paul Zanker, *The Power of Images in the Age of Augustus* (1988)

annotations.title: 'This was a man!': the Renaissance ideal mixed in Roman clay
annotations.body:
Antony's closing praise of Brutus — 'the elements / So mix'd in him that Nature might stand up / And say to all the world, This was a man!' — uses the Renaissance doctrine of the four elements (earth, water, air, fire) believed to compose the human constitution in varying proportions. A man 'well-mixed' was temperamentally balanced: the blood (air), phlegm (water), yellow bile (fire), and black bile (earth) in healthful ratio. The phrase is a paean to the ideal human.

The compliment is, finally, humanist rather than specifically Roman. 'This was a man!' — not 'this was a Roman', not 'this was a republican', but 'this was a man.' Shakespeare lets Antony step, for one line, outside political categories and grant Brutus the most general possible praise. The move is gracious and true. It is also the move by which Antony makes Brutus's republican commitment non-political — an aesthetic virtue rather than a programmatic one. The body politic of the old Rome is ended; the private virtue of Brutus is preserved as a beautiful example. Shakespeare's Antony has enacted, in eight lines, the precise cultural move by which empire absorbs republic: the politics die, the character becomes monumental.
sources: E. M. W. Tillyard, *The Elizabethan World Picture* (1943)

annotations.title: Octavius orders the funeral: the Republic ends with a soldier's burial
annotations.body:
The play's last speech is Octavius's. It is five lines of official language: a command for burial with 'all respect and rites of burial,' a soldier's order of honour, and a call to the field to rest. Shakespeare closes a 2,500-line play on the voice of the man who will, within fifteen years, be Augustus — the man whom Cassius mocked in 5.1 as a 'peevish schoolboy.'

The Republic is not argued dead here; it is administratively closed. Octavius speaks as if giving routine military orders, because that is what closing the Republic looked like at the time: a series of routine decisions, burial rites conducted, offices reassigned, a field called to rest. The audience leaves the theatre having watched the Republic end not with a coup but with a clean-up. The emotional force of the play has been invested in Brutus and Cassius; the administrative closure has been handed to Octavius.

Shakespeare's choice of how to end is specific. The older Roman-play tradition (the Senecan tradition, plays like Buchanan's *Baptistes*) would have closed on moralising choral speech. Shakespeare closes on the victor's plain order: 'So call the field to rest; and let's away, / To part the glories of this happy day.' 'Happy' is Octavius's word, not the play's. The audience feels the gap. The play argues that empire is administrative in exactly the way Octavius speaks: tidy, efficient, a little cold, the soldiers filed back to camp, the glory to be parted by the victors.

annotations.title: 'I kill'd not thee with half so good a will': the confession Brutus could not afford while living
annotations.body:
Brutus's dying half-line to Caesar's spirit is the play's most private and devastating admission. Through five acts Brutus has framed the assassination as civic necessity reluctantly discharged: 'Not that I loved Caesar less, but that I loved Rome more' (3.2). The justification depends on the ratio of love-to-principle coming out the right way — he had to love Rome *more* than Caesar. At the moment of his own death, Brutus inverts the ratio. Killing himself is easier than killing Caesar was. The love for Caesar was, in the end, the more weighty.

Shakespeare does not stage a repudiation of the assassination; Brutus does not say 'it was wrong' or 'I regret it.' He simply lets the emotional truth slip out in the one moment where performance has no further purpose. The line is private in a way the whole play has not let Brutus be private before. Every previous speech of his has been for an audience — Cassius, the conspirators, the crowd, Messala. This one is for Caesar's ghost alone, and the ghost is his own evil spirit (4.3 L276), so it is finally for Brutus himself.

The line's placement matters. It comes as Brutus is running onto Strato's sword. It is not a considered reflection; it is a confession at the instant of dying. The play is arguing that the real truth about a political act is sometimes only available at the cost of the life it took to perform the act. Stoic philosophy is supposed to provide the clarity; it doesn't. The suicide does.

trials.prompt: What does 'I kill'd not thee with half so good a will' reveal?
options: Brutus's private admission at the instant of dying: killing himself is easier than killing Caesar was — the love for Caesar was more weighty than the republican principle; the justification of 3.2 inverts

trials.prompt: Antony's 'This was the noblest Roman of them all' is read at face value as tribute. In context it is also:
options: Political theatre — Antony has just won; proscribing senators; eulogising Brutus as singular among the conspirators both honours Brutus and teaches the new regime how former enemies will be treated; a classic imperial move of absorbing republican virtue into personal monument while ending republican action

trials.prompt: The play ends with Octavius ordering Brutus's burial. What does Shakespeare achieve by closing on this voice?
options: The Republic is administratively closed, not argued dead — the man who will be Augustus speaks in the register of routine military orders; within fifteen years he will rule Rome alone, ending the Republic the conspirators died to preserve

trials.prompt: Why does Brutus invoke Caesar ('Caesar, now be still') as he dies?
options: He is laying the Ghost of Caesar — the spirit that appeared to him at Sardis (4.3) and returned at Philippi (5.5 L16); the suicide completes the prophecy and ends the haunting
He wants Caesar to hear him

trials.prompt: What has Shakespeare implied, across Acts 4 and 5, about the conspirators' political achievement?
options: Their goal failed precisely — proscription begins in 4.1, the republican armies lose at Philippi in 5.3 and 5.5, and the Republic closes with Octavius's final speech. The assassination Brutus performed to prevent monarchy delivered exactly monarchy, in Octavius, within fifteen years
The Republic was already gone before the play began

trials.prompt: 'This was a man!' as Antony's final line praises Brutus in which register?
options: Humanist / Renaissance — the four-elements doctrine of a 'well-mixed' man, temperamentally balanced; a paean to the ideal human that, by being general, makes Brutus's republican commitment non-political and safe for empire to absorb

trials.prompt: What is specifically Shakespearean about closing the play with a five-line administrative speech from Octavius?
options: It refuses Senecan choral moralising and hands the final word to the victor's plain order; empire is made to look administrative rather than dramatic — the audience leaves having watched the Republic end as clean-up
